data.title: Trip.com: Good Prospects, Undemanding Valuation
data.description:
Context

Trip.com’s (TCOM) share price has dropped 30% over the past 6 months after it was reported the company is being probed by Chinese regulators over anti-competitive practices. The resignation of the two founders did not help improve matters, and Trip.com’s share price remains relatively undervalued despite solid results. With around 20% upside, the stock offers an attractive entry point.

Business context

Trip.com is China’s largest online travel platform (OTA) with an estimated market share of over 50%. The vast majority of revenues are generated in China, where Trip.com (through their local platforms Ctrip and Qunar) competes against local players like Alibaba’s Fliggy (BABA), Meituan (MPNGF) (MPNGY), and Klook.

Revenues are derived from four key segments:

  • Accommodation reservations (commissions from hotel partners for rooms sold by Trip.com’s platforms) accounting for over 40% of revenues (as of 2025).

  • Transportation ticketing (commissions on flight and train tickets), which accounts for around a third of revenues.

  • Packaged tours (referral fees from transport and hotel partners), which account for under a tenth of revenues.

  • Corporate travel (commission from transport ticketing, accommodation services, and packaged tours sold to corporate clients), which accounts for around 5% of revenues.

Financial performance

In FY 2025, revenues rose 17% YoY to CNY 62.4 billion. Accommodation reservation revenues rose 21% YoY to CNY 26 billion, while transportation ticketing revenues rose 11% YoY to CNY 22.5 billion. Packaged tour revenues rose 8% YoY to CNY 4.7 billion. Corporate travel revenues rose 13% YoY to CNY 2.8 billion.

Net income was CNY 33 billion, but that was largely driven by a nearly CNY 20 billion gain from a one-time gain from the sale of their stake in MakeMyTrip in India. Adjusted for this gain, 2025 net income would have been lower than the CNY 16 billion net income generated in 2024 (after adjusting for the CNY 1.1 billion gain during the year). The FY 2025 net income drop appears to have been driven by an increase in the cost of revenues (up 21% YoY), sales & marketing expenses (up 25% YoY), and product development expenses (up 15% YoY).

Prospects

Two potential growth drivers stand out for TCOM in the medium to long term.

Domestic tourism has room to grow, and Trip.com (through Ctrip) is well positioned

A few factors could drive domestic travel going forward; Chinese policymakers are looking to stimulate domestic demand to support economic growth, which could be a boon for China’s travel sector, and with their ‘Common Prosperity’ aspirations boosting incomes across urban and rural populations, broad based demand for domestic travel could see continued growth momentum. In 2025, domestic trips made by urban residents rose 14% YoY to nearly 5 billion trips, while trips made by rural residents rose 21% YoY to over 1.5 billion trips. Ctrip, as the largest player in domestic travel, is well positioned to benefit. The company has also already strategically established their presence in rural China, which not only aligns with Common Prosperity policy goals but also captures domestic travel spending flowing towards rural destinations. To illustrate, Ctrip’s “Beautiful Countryside” program has digitized thousands of rural homestays and tourism operators and more than doubled rural tourism incomes since 2021.

Domestic travel in China grew 16% in 2025, with 6.5 billion domestic travel trips made during the year, translating into approximately 6 trips per person annually. This is lower than the U.S. where over 2 billion trips are taken domestically, translating into nearly 8 trips per person annually. 6 trips per person with per capita incomes of USD 13,000 looks considerably high compared to America's 8 trips per person with per capita incomes of over USD 50,000. This is likely because the vast majority of domestic trips in China are through rail (I estimate rail accounts for over 50% of domestic trips), which is relatively low cost compared to flying (rail is as much as 70% cheaper than flying) and hassle-free compared to driving, in contrast to the U.S. where passenger rail is extremely limited and where automobiles or planes are the most popular mode of transport.

Assuming per capita incomes increase at a mid single digit rate over the next decade, per capita incomes in China could grow by as much as 50% from current levels propelling domestic travel in terms of trip volume as well as potentially trip value through upgraded travel; in my view the vast majority of Trip.com’s 600 million users spend on cheaper travel modes such as rail - OTA take rates for accommodation are around 15%-25% for accommodation, 8%-10% for flight tickets, and around 2%-3% for rail tickets, if we assume very roughly a blended take rate of 7%, a back-of-the napkin calculation would suggest Trip.com’s GMV is around CNY 800 billion which translates into roughly CNY 1,500 for each of their 600 million users, an extremely low figure. For perspective, Booking.com’s GMV of USD 186 billion generated from 130 million users translates into USD 1,400 or nearly CNY 10,000.

A corresponding 50% or so increase in spend on travel (of which domestic travel is likely to remain a major beneficiary) does not look far fetched which suggests Trip.com could potentially see revenues potentially increase by 50% as well from current levels, assuming market share remains unchanged. Trip.com’s Chinese platform Ctrip’s ticketing business includes train tickets in addition to airline tickets, and they are thus positioned to capture a share of any increase in domestic travel demand, be it rail or airplane.

Well positioned to capitalize on international tourist arrivals

Inbound tourism is another pillar of the Chinese government’s strategy to stimulate travel and thereby drive domestic consumption. From expanding their visa-free scheme to smoother tax refunds for shoppers, the government is aggressively offering incentives to drive inbound travel. China saw over 30 million foreign tourist arrivals in 2025, up nearly 50% YoY, and there is likely enormous room for further growth considering neighboring countries such as Malaysia, Thailand, and Japan alone receive over 30 million foreign tourists each

Trip.com, which saw international bookings on its international platform rise 60% YoY in 2025 and served 20 million inbound travelers to China, is in prime position to benefit as the largest player with a growing international presence. Trip.com already has a presence in over 39-40 countries unlike rivals like Fliggy or Meituan, which are primarily China based. Meanwhile, their scale and extensive network of Chinese vendor partners make them a far more competitive choice over rivals like Booking.com or Expedia.

The U.S. saw around 68 million international tourist arrivals in 2025, more than double the number of visitors to China. The figure was as high as 80 million pre-Covid, reflecting ample room for Chinese international tourist arrivals to grow. Trip.com’s international business contributes about 40% to revenue/bookings, up from 35% in 2024. If we assume this business doubles, largely on the back of growing interest in tourism in China, this could become a CNY 40 billion plus business going forward. While margins are currently seeing slight compression as Trip.com ramps up their international infrastructure and marketing spend, this could be offset longer term as international tourists likely yield higher commissions as relatively higher spenders than domestic tourists.

Valuation

If we assume Trip.com grows revenues from CNY 62 billion to CNY 100 billion (or around 10% annually which looks reasonable considering they have been growing by double digits and management projects 14% growth for fiscal 2026) over the medium term i.e., 5 years (being CNY 24 billion from a doubling of their international revenues and a 50% increase in revenues from their domestic business), net margins increasing slightly to around 21% suggests at the end of year 5 Trip.com would be generating around CNY 21 billion in earnings. If we add a P/E of 14 (Trip.com’s cost of is equity 7%) which is not unreasonable considering Booking.com has a P/E of around 25, Trip.com would be worth CNY 294 billion, higher than the CNY 238 billion market cap today, a roughly 20% upside.

Risks

AI concerns

Much has been said about AI potentially devaluing OTA’s position as a traffic aggregator as travelers turn to agentic AI for bookings. This is a risk, but at this stage, the risk looks low; for direct bookings with vendors themselves, and that too for larger ones such as chain hotels, there may be a possibility, but more likely than not, travelers may want to compare options and make informed decisions as opposed to outsourcing the entire decision to an AI agent.

Competition and regulatory scrutiny are a concern

Trip.com’s share price suffered a major setback when it was reported that the company was being investigated by Chinese regulators for monopolistic practices. While I doubt the company will be liable to crippling fines, the possibility of Trip.com ceding market share to competitors like Fliggy, Meituan, WeChat (TCEHY) cannot be ruled out, which in turn could result in a meaningful impact on margins if Trip.com’s take rate suffers and marketing spend increases to curb market share erosion. Trip.com, in my view, has a narrow moat in its unrivaled scale as China’s biggest OTA, which likely allows for greater negotiating power on commission and other operating terms with vendor partners, and any significant impact to market share could impact overall competitiveness, profitability (net margins may shrink below the projected 21%), and therefore their share price.

Takeaway

Trip.com has a buy analyst consensus rating. Despite good growth prospects, reports of regulatory investigations have spooked investors, and Trip.com’s share price appears moderately undervalued. The stock offers an interesting entry point and could be viewed as a buy for investors looking for exposure to China’s tourism growth story.

data.title: Rocket Lab's Story Just Changed
data.description:
Rocket Lab Corporation (RKLB) finds itself in a very different place than it did even a year ago, and I think the market is having trouble processing the extent of that change. We are no longer dealing with a small launch disruptor trying to prove itself. We are increasingly a space infrastructure play with more and more exposure to defense programs and satellite systems.

That’s a great strategy, but it also means the standard against which I evaluate the business has changed. Previously, the fact that the company was making progress at all was the key. That’s no longer the case. In fact, the more progress the company makes, the more I find myself asking whether or not that progress is actually translating into something scalable, repeatable, and ultimately defensible at the levels we’re seeing. What’s relevant now isn’t the direction. It’s the execution.

The Real Story Is Revenue Composition, Not Revenue Growth

Revenue growth, as you know, remains strong. But frankly, that’s not particularly insightful, at least to me. What’s more relevant is the manner in which the company is generating that revenue growth. Space Systems now represents around 58% ($103.8/180 million) of total revenue, which represents a fundamental shift in the way the business operates. Launch remains the narrative, but Space Systems remains where the revenue visibility and stability are actually occurring. To me, the way I think about this is less about diversification and more about the way the company defines itself.

In other words, the way I see it, the company is increasingly becoming a defense-oriented contractor with integrated launch capabilities rather than a launch disruptor. That difference has important implications. Defenses tend to have more predictable revenue growth but also tend to have lower growth rates. They generally don't command the same type of multiple expansion as the early-stage launch narrative.

Yet Rocket Lab seems to be benefiting from both interpretations. I have trouble wrapping my head around that. It implies that the market is still operating under the old narrative but is also benefiting from the advantages of the new narrative.

Why Neutron Now Carries the Weight

Allow me to clarify that I don't think management has done anything wrong in terms of execution. The Electron continues to prove itself to be reliable and trustworthy. Backlog is increasing to $1.85 billion. Contract wins have been good and reinforce the credibility of the organization, especially in government programs.

However, I'm not as concerned about what's been delivered as much as what needs to be delivered. The expectations that have been priced into this stock have moved materially. What used to be upside is now partially assumed. This is an important point. Execution is no longer good enough.

I believe that Neutron is what changes the game for Rocket Lab. It changes the market opportunity and changes the economics. Furthermore, it changes the competitive position of Rocket Lab as it becomes a competitor in the medium-lift launch market.

A transition into the second half of 2026 based on a manufacturing-related issue is something that does not alter my opinion on the long-term potential. Aerospace development is rarely linear, and I would much rather see delays than compromised reliability. What I see is a longer period of time when we, as an investing community, are betting on success without validation.

This is where I start to get nervous. There is a difference between believing in something and betting on it as a sure thing. I believe, as an investing community, that we are betting on inevitability at the current valuations. We can see the potential if Neutron is successful. We can see the potential if they fall behind or ramp up more slowly than we anticipate.

Cash Burn Reflects Reality, Not Optionality

The next area where I believe there’s a disconnect is cash flow. The company continues to burn cash, and this is largely driven by Neutron development and infrastructure spending. This is the expected result. I don’t believe there’s anything to worry about in the near term with around $1 billion in cash and cash equivalents. However, I do believe the market is treating this cash as a safety net, not as a burn rate that will be spent. There’s a material difference between these two statements.

The company hasn’t demonstrated operating leverage yet. Until then, the cash burn isn’t something you can dismiss as a temporary state. It’s a permanent part of the story. What I believe is more relevant than the cash burn rate is whether or not this will start to translate into leverage. That’s still in the future.

When Sky-High Multiples Meet Even Higher Expectations

To me, valuation is ultimately a function of how much of that long-term revenue ramp I believe in today. At a rough 52x 2026 price/sales, coming into the mid-30s in 2027 and high teens by 2030, this is a rich stock on near-term financials. There is no getting around that.

What adds complexity to this is the underlying growth. The revenue is supposed to accelerate from 2026’s ~$850 million to over $3.7 billion in 2031. That kind of growth can support high valuation multiples. However, that growth must be achieved.

If I use a more reasonable valuation approach of 15x to 20x on 2030 revenue estimates of ~$2.4 billion, I can see potential for success. However, that is dependent on a number of factors. This is not a cheap stock. It is a stock that is already factoring in significant growth.

The Neutron Timeline Is the Real Pressure Point

At the end of the day, we are dealing with a Neutron story, and I believe that the investor base is underestimating the sensitivity of the schedule. The tank issue may be fixed, and in fact, it’s helping to support the thesis because delays have a way of creeping into all things.

The thing that actually concerns me more, however, is the ripple effect. So, if we have delays, we know we’re going to have a ripple effect on the revenue growth, but we’re also going to have a ripple effect on the space systems, where we already have a growth profile based on coordination of the programs. That’s where the variability kicks in. The question isn’t the demand. It’s the ability of Rocket Lab to deliver on the backlog without the timing pressures building.

Bottom Line

I think Rocket Lab is a high-risk/reward profile story that is time-dependent. The story is real with the defense component, the vertical integration, and the ultimate scale.

data.title: Rates Spark: ECB And BoE Meet Hawkish Markets
data.description:
By Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist and Michiel Tukker, Senior European Rates Strategist

The situation in the Middle East remains highly volatile, as underscored by events over the past day. They turned a day that started on a calmer note into one marked by another spike in energy prices.

The Fed kept rates on hold as expected and continued to project a cut in 2026 and 2027. However, a hawkish tinge came with the revision to higher GDP growth over the forecast horizon and a small lift in the long-run Fed funds rate.

In sum, 2y Treasury yields rose 10bp on the day, and 10y yields were lifted above 4.25% by these events, with the market further paring back its expectations for Fed cuts. The first cut is now fully discounted by summer next year.

ECB's Lagarde could sound more hawkish, but can she be more hawkish than the market?

The market is also going into today’s European Central Bank meeting with more aggressive rate hike expectations on the back of the latest geopolitical escalation - two hikes are again fully priced in by year-end.

No one expects the ECB to act today, and given that the ECB’s new forecast will have been overtaken by events already, the main focus will be on the guidance provided by President Lagarde. Even if good arguments can be made to look through the turmoil, it is very unlikely that hikes will be taken off the table at this stage. So the question will be rather to what degree Lagarde can keep pace with the already hawkish market pricing. In the end, signalling a readiness to act and a data-dependent and meeting-by-meeting approach to assessing the appropriate policy stance will likely mean that energy prices will remain the overriding factor for market rates for now.

As for the negative feedback loop that we think could still rein in too hawkish pricing, we have seen risk markets weakening again yesterday but not really tipping over yet. And long-end real yields were still not signalling longer-term growth concerns. European government bond spreads, like the 10y BTP/Bund remained below the prior week’s peaks, with Bund spreads to swaps still showing only a muted reaction to the situation.

Watch the BoE vote split as markets guess the reaction function

As markets are trying to guess the reaction function of central banks, the vote split for the Bank of England will be even more closely watched than usual. Markets have turned 180 degrees over the past few weeks, from pricing in two cuts this year to one hike. We think contemplating a hike is premature, but energy prices will have to move lower to ease markets. The BoE’s meeting will make any future move conditional on energy prices, but the vote split could communicate a more nuanced stance. Our economist takes 7-2 in favour of no change as the base case, but be prepared for surprises.

Whilst UK rates have been hit relatively strongly by the surge in energy prices, we don’t see a higher risk premium for holding gilts. During last year’s periods of market stress, we noticed that gilts often suffered more than Bunds. But whilst the spreads between many European government bonds and the swap curve have widened materially since February, this is not obvious for gilts. The rise in the 10y gilt yield has been a function of a hawkish repricing of the BoE and not by the swap spreads. We would therefore really have to see a repricing of policy rates to see gilt yields ease lower.

Thursday’s events and market view

The main focus of the day is the ECB and BoE policy-setting meetings. Although no changes are expected today, the guidance provided will be key in judging the reaction functions amid the geopolitical turmoil. The morning started with the release of the UK unemployment rate and average earnings data for January. US data later in the day covers housing data with building permits and new home sales for January, as well as the jobs market with the usual weekly jobless claims data.

In primary markets, Spain auctions 5y, 7y, and 10y bonds for up to €6bn. France taps short- to medium-term bonds as well as inflation-linked bonds for a total of up to €14bn.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: MPLX: Strong Income Floor With Visible Growth Upside
data.description:
I have analyzed aggressive stress scenarios for MPLX (MPLX) in this thesis and still find the combination of high income, visible growth potential, and a protected floor hard to ignore even under stress. The story may have evolved from a rerating thesis to an income play with decent growth compounding, but it still presents a Strong Buy case in my view.

A look at the price charts and rolling 1-year total returns shows that MPLX is not the cheapest value play today compared to 2022-23. At the same time, the current 1-year return is not stretched either (~15.5%). This is not a stock that has stretched its valuations in 2025. The yield has compressed from ~9% to ~7% in the same period. That remains still lucrative despite the price jump. So investors considering fresh entries today are not entering at the highest phase of MPLX's momentum in the recent past. And as we will see, with a strong floor argument, MPLX is likely to be a great income plus growth play even at current levels.

The Rerating Story and Stress Scenario Today

The forward EV to EBITDA ratio is currently at ~11x - that is around ~25% up from early 2024 levels. The TTM ratio is up by a similar ~30% from the 2022-23 lows. That is the extent of rerating that has happened, and the numbers we will use to test a stress floor for valuations applicable today.

Now let us construct a downside scenario from here on, from just the valuation perspective, and then we will layer in other factors through the thesis. If we simply assume the 2022 valuation floor is today's stress case (reasonable worst-case assumption), then we could see 20-25% corrections due to multiple compression alone. Now here is the income cushion math. Currently at 7%, the distribution is guided to grow at 12.5% over the next 2 years. So a ~20% downside in a stress scenario is recoverable through income alone in 2-2.5 years.

This is what I view as an aggressive stress case for several reasons. One, unlike typical late-cycle midstream setups, fundamentals have scaled alongside valuation. Since 2022, adjusted EBITDA has grown at a CAGR of ~6.7%, and distributable cash flow has grown by ~6.1%. So the rerating is not just excess in a liquidity driven rally - it is earnings-backed. A reversal to 2022 valuations may not mean a 20% correction.

Risk Assessment

The second reason why the 2022 stress multiples are overly aggressive today is because the external and macro risks are still relatively contained. 2022 was one of the sharpest rate hike phases in history, leading to violent repricing of yield assets. Today rates are plateauing, and the odds of a shock remain much lower. From a geopolitical standpoint, we had a Ukraine war shock in 2022. There were fears of energy market dislocation and broader supply chain repercussions that led to extreme volatility.

The Iran shock today could turn out to be a greater shock, but I view it as an indirect macro shock, not a business model shock for MPLX. We are now looking at inflation pressures, oil supply disruptions, and crude spikes - not direct factors that impact MPLX's infrastructure business model that is volume-based (not price-based). In fact, there could be more positives emerging if producers increase activity and more volumes flow through in response. MPLX is primarily present in US onshore basins and stays insulated from the disruption in Middle East supply chains. Overall, the Iran risk presents the possibility of a deeper crisis that can indirectly mean broader market risk-off sentiments or even extreme sell-offs, but it is hard to model it as either a bigger or smaller risk on its own than the Ukraine crisis in 2022-23 - especially for MPLX's intrinsic model.

There are other factors that look less extreme for MPLX today. That includes an inflation spike that introduced margin uncertainty and cost overruns for projects. Today inflation is moderating, capex visibility is higher, and projects have clearer and more predictable cost structures. We are already seeing rotation away from AI, high valuations, and long duration assumptions toward cash flow and yield based real assets that could actually benefit MPLX's thesis.

The company-specific risk factors today also appear contained. There is a worry around overcapacity when it comes to LPG exports, ranging from lower utilization to weaker returns. But the projects MPLX is building are backed by real demand (LNG growth, petrochemicals) and are expected to be filled at startup, as discussed during the Q4 earnings call. We may see timing delays at worst, but not an earnings hit.

Volume risk is even weaker. The Marcellus system is running at ~97% utilization. That means demand already exceeds capacity. Finally, the balance sheet looks strong. Leverage is ~3.7x with a ~4x cap, and distributable cash flow is ~$5.8b annually. The current dividends imply ~$4.3 annual payouts per share. With ~1.02b outstanding shares, the dividend obligation of ~$4.4b annually is well covered. That leaves only ~$1.4b internally, while growth capex is running above $2b. So distributions alone don’t comfortably fund both payouts and growth. The gap is funded through a mix of balance sheet capacity (leverage still ~3.7x, below the 4x targets) and JV structures. At the same time, management is guiding 12.5% distribution growth for two years, which could compress coverage toward ~1.15–1.2x. That looks tight but should still be covered, as growth capex today is tied to mid-teens return projects coming online from 2026 onwards, which should lift EBITDA and DCF. The only risk that we need to monitor is major project timing lapses.

The Upside

I have spent a lot of time stress testing MPLX against a 2022-type downside and risks to the thesis, and have hopefully been able to show that risks are contained and income more than offsets downside. But that framing so far misses something important - the business itself is materially stronger today and shows a lot of potential growth opportunities too. And that changes what fair valuation should look like.

I have already talked about how EBITDA and DCF have changed. Capital allocation also appears more disciplined and structured today. Growth is funded, but not at the cost of balance sheet stress. And income appears to be prioritized, going by the 12.5% distribution guidance for the next 2 years.

High overall utilization rates show the current system is already close to capacity constrained. That signals a strong demand backdrop. The growth projects will therefore likely meet the mid-teens return targets. Management has explicitly indicated more than 15% US natural gas demand growth through 2030, driven by LNG exports and rising power demand (especially data centers). That’s important because this is not cyclical demand alone. LNG capacity is already under construction, and data center power load is a multi-year buildout, not a cyclical or short term spike. On the NGL side, the Q4 call highlights global petrochemical demand (ethane/propane) and export growth as key drivers. This ties directly into MPLX’s strategy - fractionation and LPG export terminals.

Overall, looking at the demand strength, projects like Harmon Creek III or Secretariat II are not about hoping for volumes - they are about removing supply bottlenecks. The same is the theme in the Permian, where we are seeing rising gas-to-oil ratios (more associated gas per barrel) and strong demand for takeaway capacity (pipeline expansions, Eiger, BANGL, etc.).

Adding the growth upside to the thesis, we are not only looking at the ~7-8% yield over the next few years, but some growth compounding too from the mid-teens returns expected from the growth projects. That comfortably pushes the total return expectations into double digits, while the downside cushion is extremely strong (where I have spent a bulk of my time in this thesis). The Strong Buy rating for MPLX is not an indication of a huge expectation of rerating ahead or stellar returns. It is more a vote for MPLX's risk-adjusted return profile. The view should be primarily an income asset with a strong floor and moderate growth optionality with high odds.

data.title: Hon Hai Precision: Strong Q4 Results And Positive Outlook
data.description:
I've kept Hon Hai Precision Industry Co., Ltd. (HNHPF) (2317.TW) as a Buy-rated name.

I'm impressed with its above-consensus quarterly results and management's expectations of a rising ROE.

The long runway of HNHPF's core segments was detailed in my earlier November 25, 2025, write-up.

Operating Profitability Exceeded Expectations

HNHPF published a set of slides this week unveiling its latest financials.

The group's YoY “Operating Profit/OP” expansion improved from 3Q2025's +28.8% to 4Q2025's +32.7%. According to S&P Capital IQ, its Oct-Dec '25 OP of NT$85.6B came in 9% above the sell-side's NT$78,227M projection.

My take is that the outperformance is attributable to both top-line growth and margin enhancement.

HNHPF's turnover for the recent three-month period was 22.3% higher year-on-year at NT$2.61T. The QoQ increase of 26.6% also beat management's prior guidance of +4-15%.

The “Cloud and Networking” or “AI servers” unit was a standout. This business's 4Q25 sales jumped 99% YoY to account for a leading 42% of the company-wide figure.

I'd already expressed my optimism about its AI server operations in the late-Nov '25 piece. I stressed then, “The rise of Sovereign AI will be a major tailwind.” I reasoned that “governments around the world will naturally feel safer working with the strongest and most reputable players,” like HNHPF.

Furthermore, the enterprise's EBIT-to-revenue widened by 0.3 ppts year-on-year to 3.3% for the final quarter of last year. That was 10 bps ahead of the consensus estimate based on S&P Capital IQ.

I believe fixed-cost leverage boosted HNHPF's profitability.

Its 4Q2025 OpEx went up modestly by 2% to NT$67.7B on YoY terms. That's much less than the low-twenties percent surge in top-line during the same timeframe.

Anticipating Solid Revenues And Better Margins

The positive momentum from last year should be sustained into the new fiscal period.

The company is guiding for its turnover to rise by over +15% in the first three months of FY26. This would mark a five-year peak for 1st quarter YoY increases.

HNHPF has traditionally focused more on GPU-based AI servers. In my opinion, the ongoing diversification into “Application Specific Integrated Circuit” or “ASIC” systems will be an important lever.

At the analyst briefing (S&P Capital IQ transcript), the analyst emphasized that “we expect our ASIC server business to maintain multiple-fold growth momentum in 2026.”

GPU-related operations contributed roughly nine-tenths of its FY2025 “Cloud and Networking” sales. But the “overall GPU to ASIC market structure is 80:20,” according to Q4 call disclosures. It's reasonable to infer that a gradual alignment with industry mix could lead to a doubling of the firm's ASIC revenue contribution from 10% to 20%.

HNHPF recently participated in JPMorgan Chase & Co.'s (JPM) investor event in the current week. JPM sees the group “adding one more client (to 3) this year in the ASIC business.” This assessment is supported by conversations with management suggesting “more (ASIC-focused) projects are coming online.” The information is taken from the bank's March 16 report (not publicly available) titled “Key Takeaways from CEO-CFO Conference.”

It's also targeting a similarly robust mid-teens revenue improvement for the full year. I think that's realistic with the GPU-to-ASIC transition in mind.

Separately, I'm predicting that HNHPF's “Gross Profit Margin/GPM” will go from FY2025's 6.1% to FY2026's 6.3%.

The group shared at the results meeting, “We have shifted part of our server transaction model from buy and sell to a consignment model.” The rationale is that “the buy and sell transaction model for high-value AI servers negatively affects gross margin.”

It was also disclosed at the mid-March JPM forum that the ASIC sub-segment boasts “a lower proportion of buy-and-sell.”

I have confidence in HNHPF's margin prospects, considering the favorable change in revenue composition.

ROE Goal Warrants A Re-rating

The firm registered a “Return On Equity/ROE” of 11.3% in the previous fiscal year. That's up 1.6 ppts vis-à-vis FY24.

It has established a new 12 percent target post-4Q25 earnings. I take the view that this is attainable. HNHPF has proven itself with encouraging performance and guidance. The analyst call commentary pointed to “vertical integration of components” and “economies of scale expand” as key drivers.

As per S&P Capital IQ, the stock currently trades at 1.44x FY27 price-to-book.

My fair-value P/B for HNHPF is 1.80 times using the “Gordon Growth Model/GGM” formula. The inputs are a 12% ROE, an 8% cost of equity (market average), and a 3% growth (developed economy proxy).

Its potential upside is +25% (1.8/1.44).

You Need To Weigh These Bear-Case Scenarios

The “Cloud and Networking” division might perform poorly, assuming that the customers' major AI initiatives are deferred or even canceled.

A failure to shift its topline structure towards higher-margin sources could translate into lower-than-expected profitability for the future.

My Concluding Remarks

I'm staying bullish on this name.

You would expect investors to reward HNHPF with a more demanding valuation. Its ROE is on a structural uptrend, judging by its recent numbers.

data.title: High Tide: Remexian Recovers, Canna Cabana Momentum Fair
data.description:
High Tide Inc. (HITI) reported the company’s fiscal Q1 results from the November-January period on the 17th of March. The cannabis retailer and distributor is showing a promising recovery in the acquired Remexian business after a slow start, but the core Canadian retail business had a noticeably slower quarter due to a market slowdown. Overall, the High Tide story remains on track after the report, but Canadian cannabis consumption has to be watched closely going forward.

I remained at a Strong Buy rating in my previous January article on the stock, titled “High Tide: Why The Market Is Wrong After Q4.” The stock has since returned 9%, while the S&P 500 has declined by -4%.

High Tide Q1 Review

In my opinion, High Tide reported good, but not great, fiscal Q1 results. Revenues came in at C$178.3 million, up by 25% through the acquisition of Remexian as well as organic growth at Canna Cabana. Adjusted EBITDA reached C$11.5 million, up by 62% year-on-year through solid margin gains. Behind good overall surface-level financials, High Tide’s segment performance was more mixed as EBITDA did decline slightly from Q4.

The most important segment, brick-and-mortar retail, had a noticeably slower quarter after very strong momentum in the past. Same-store sales growth only came in at 0.5%, below inflation and compared to 4.1% in FY2025 and even faster growth in later quarters of the prior fiscal year. Canna Cabana’s store expansion still led total brick-and-mortar revenues to grow by 10% to C$149.7 million, but slower same-store sales are quick to pressure earnings. Despite a 10 basis point sequential gain in the gross margin, brick-and-mortar segment EBITDA came in at C$13.2 million, down by C$0.9 million sequentially through SG&A deleverage.

Brick-and-mortar earnings were still up considerably year-on-year from thin Q1'FY2025 margins.

While the brick-and-mortar performance was slower, for now, the slowdown seems to be macro-driven and doesn’t signal slowing brand momentum for Canna Cabana. Industry data reflects a clear hiccup in cannabis sales in Ontario and Alberta, Canna Cabana’s two most important provinces, in November and December. For the two months, the two provinces showed a -2.4% decline in consumption compared to much better growth in preceding months. Competitor SNDL Inc.’s (SNDL) cannabis same-store sales even declined by -0.7% in the October-December quarter. SNDL relates the slowdown to a market slowdown.

Further, High Tide notes extremely harsh weather in Ontario during January, slowing down sales in High Tide’s fiscal Q1 period. Canna Cabana’s momentum was still fair when considering industry conditions, in my opinion. For now, the underlying growth story remains on track as Cabana Club continues to attract new members, white-label sales slowly grow in penetration, and the company continues to open new stores.

I believe that the focus is on a market recovery in Canadian cannabis consumption given the current slowdown; a prolonged market slowdown could continue to pressure upcoming brick-and-mortar earnings. Stable industry sales are still ultimately needed to sustain great store economics. Relatively slow consumer confidence, and a seeming uptick in illegal sales, according to earnings call remarks, shadow the forward legal consumption outlook for FY2026.

The second most important segment’s performance was much more encouraging. Distribution operations, representing Remexian, started to stabilize gradually after a weak Q4. The medical cannabis distribution segment’s revenues came in at C$25.0 million, and the gross margin improved by 520 basis points sequentially to 9.2%, even though supply chain issues in Portugal still carried on into Q1. While segment adjusted EBITDA was again near breakeven at -C$0.3 million, a further recovery should push earnings positive going forward. High Tide notes that February distribution revenues were C$12 million and the gross margin was 20%. Biomass procured from Canada at greater margins should start to improve earnings from Q2 forward.

High Tide is now even looking to enter the U.K. market through M&A in the following months; the company has a clear ambition to capture a share of the European market. It seems that procurement synergies from Canadian retail operations make international wholesale operations very lucrative to expand into.

Lastly, e-commerce segment financials improved very slightly sequentially but remain poor overall. Revenues came in at C$6.7 million, and adjusted EBITDA was deeply negative at C$1.5 million. The segment’s performance needs to show a much faster rebound to become profitable. Luckily, e-commerce losses are moderate in comparison to High Tide's total earnings.

High Tide’s Valuation Is Still Attractive

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for High Tide. For more thorough explanations and notes, I refer to my previous articles on the stock.

I estimate similar overall financials as previously. I lowered the FY2026 revenue estimate due to the market slowdown in Canadian consumption, but slightly raised subsequent years’ growth estimates as Remexian’s growth now seems more confident. The FY2026-FY2035 organic revenue CAGR is set at 5.9%.

While the consumption slowdown and Remexian’s supply chain issues temporarily slow down margin progress, I again estimate the EBITDA margin to ultimately stabilize at 10%. I expect similar cash flows over the long term, but assume slower free cash flow in the next three years due to Remexian’s increasing working capital needs.

The estimates put High Tide’s fair value at $4.38, implying 79% upside as of writing. I believe that High Tide’s valuation remains attractive after the Q1 report; long-term earnings potential is undervalued even though the stock doesn’t currently trade at very cheap earnings multiples.

The fair value estimate is down from $4.67 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 9.33%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I again estimate a high 12% interest rate and a modest 10% long-term debt-to-equity ratio. For the cost of equity, I use Canada’s 10-year bond yield of 3.40% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.23% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for Canada. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.94. With an ESG add-on of 1.5% and a liquidity premium of 0.5%, the cost of equity stands at 9.38% and the WACC at 9.33%.

Takeaway

High Tide reported solid overall earnings growth for Q1, but the performance was split between businesses. Brick-and-mortar operations showed a hiccup as Canadian cannabis consumption slowed down, meanwhile Remexian is starting to recover from supply chain issues in Portugal. The e-commerce segment has started to stabilize, but still generates heavy losses.

There’s still great upside in HITI stock at the current valuation. As a market slowdown in Canada does pose a clear risk for upcoming quarters, though, I downgrade my rating to Buy to be slightly more cautious. I continue to hold my personal position.

data.title: Ranking Datadog's Moat As AI Rewrites The Rules For Software Investors
data.description:
In recent months, a wide variety of B2B software companies have seen their stocks cut by 30%, 40%, 50%, 60%, or even 70%, as AI agents like Claude Code have disrupted the software engineering landscape and reduced the marginal cost of producing software to zero.

In an environment like that, what separates winners from losers, and recurring revenue streams from disruptable business models?

Recently, I watched an interview with Gokul Rajaram and Harry Stebbings, where Gokul, an early employee of Google (GOOGL) (GOOG), Meta (META), Square (XYZ), and DoorDash (DASH), breaks down the actual competitive moats that he thinks will drive a business to succeed or fail through a period of AI disruption.

I found the framework useful, and in this article (and a number of following articles), I'll break down a number of top B2B SaaS companies and rank them across this matrix.

At this point, we all understand the attractiveness of software; it's a capital-light business with extremely high gross margins, strong recurring revenues, and a high level of predictability.

What matters now - as AI agents develop - is whether or not these businesses can survive going forward and continue producing fast-growing, high-margin profit flows for investors.

In this article, I'm going to be looking at Datadog (DDOG), the industry-leading monitoring platform that allows engineering teams to spot performance issues and rectify them via real-time software.

The company counts OpenAI as its largest customer, and appears set to capitalize on potential adoption by a new wave of vibe-coded software products. But how will this firm actually rank? Is the stock worth buying?

Today, I'll analyze Datadog's competitive moat, look at the business's financials and valuation, and give my take on whether or not the stock is worth buying for your portfolio.

Sound good? Let's dive in.

Defensibility

As promised, let's take a look at Datadog through the lens of competitive moats that define whether or not a business will be able to withstand competition and disruption from AI and other businesses in the future.

In his interview, Rajaram highlights nine key competitive moats that businesses can leverage to gain true defensibility:

  1. Data
  2. Workflow
  3. Regulatory
  4. Distribution
  5. Ecosystem
  6. Network
  7. Physical infrastructure
  8. Scale
  9. Brand

In the interview, Rajaram highlights that scale, brand, and physical infrastructure are not particularly relevant for B2B software companies, as companies that write software are no longer able to do so with a cost advantage (scale), with owned infrastructure, or with a brand that particularly matters to purchasing managers.

As such, I'll rank Datadog across the first six dimensions.

Moat One: Data

This is perhaps one of Datadog's strongest competitive advantages. With a platform that ingests 'trillions of data points, billions of traces, and exabytes of logs,' plus additional context such as user sessions, data lineage, and key trace histories, Datadog has a significant data advantage against competitors.

This is what is enabling the company to train its proprietary AI models, including Toto, a model focused on time-series forecasting that was trained on a dataset three times larger than the largest public alternative:

Now if you look for the largest public data set of time series data, you get to about 300 billion data points, and it covers domains that you'd expect, finance, health care, energy, transportation, some web traffic and so on.

But we train Toto on 3x that amount. And the vast majority of the data we have, we use for training, for pretraining actually, is completely unique to Datadog.

This allows Datadog's product to be significantly better than competitors at deployment monitoring, which should lead to stronger user lock-in and easier user acquisition going forward.

Points: +1

Moat Two: Workflow

Workflow is also a strong point for the company. With everyone from traditional B2B SaaS companies to consumer companies like OpenAI using Datadog, the company has embedded itself into the daily operational workflows of key engineering and security teams. When an incident happens, engineers open the Datadog platform, and the dashboard spans the entire incident lifecycle, from alert, through investigation, to resolution.

Additionally, new apps and businesses that could come online as a result of the decreasing cost of code will still need software to help them monitor their own deployments in real time, and there's no reason to suspect that Datadog will not be the go-to choice.

Points: +1

Moat Three: Regulatory

While Datadog benefits from regulatory requirements, especially in the financial sector, that require audit and compliance capabilities primarily through log monitoring, there are no key licenses that the company holds that allow it to do things that competitors cannot.

Points: +0

Moat Four: Distribution

I primarily understand 'distribution' as a business having a zero-cost way of getting in front of potential customers. In other words, a significant user base makes it easier to drive user acquisition and engagement.

While Datadog has a world-class sales team and go-to-market strategy, the platform does not have any built-in advantages that give it an edge over other corporate sales teams.

Points: +0

Moat Five: Ecosystem

An ecosystem moat means that a company has an ecosystem built on top of it that generates value for its users. This is a moat because when vendors, builders, or creators adopt a platform, they have sunk costs associated with that, and more extensive functionality increases user value. Shopify (SHOP) is a perfect example of an ecosystem company. On top of Shopify's core offering lies an 'App Store,' and most users adopt some number of apps that extend the functionality of the base platform.

Datadog has a number of integrations that allow it to span most places that businesses or individuals would deploy an app. However, Datadog itself does not have a built-in ecosystem with vendors that extend the core platform functionality while generating their own economics.

Points: +0.5

Moat Six: Network

A network moat exists when a company can bring together buyers and sellers in the same place and take economics from every transaction. Uber (UBER) is an example of a network, and so is Meta. Networks get better when more users join, either as buyers, sellers, or consumers. On this front, Datadog is a pure-play software business that grants access to users when they pay. There are no network advantages that Datadog can leverage.

Points: +0

___

Add up Datadog's point total, and you get 2.5. While this is a relatively respectable score, Rajaram mentions that he thinks businesses with three- or four-point moats are very defensible, and businesses with less are at risk of some disruption.

In my view, Datadog having OpenAI, a key disruptor, as a customer shows the value and difficulty of building the company's offering on your own. Thus, from a workflow and data perspective, Datadog will likely survive the AI disruption wave, but weaker scores on network, ecosystem, distribution, and regulation mean that competition will always exist in the space.

Financials

So, Datadog is a relatively defensible company within the software space, scoring 2.5 'moat' points.

But how are the company's financials?

In short, Datadog has a strong business across infrastructure monitoring, log management, and application performance monitoring, with each of these verticals generating at least $1 billion in annual recurring revenue.

Additionally, with trailing twelve-month gross profit margins of roughly 80%, the business is performing well, and management has considerable leverage when it comes to capital allocation:

Over the last 12 months, the business produced a negative operating margin of roughly -1.25%, but this number is skewed given the massive R&D expenses that the company spent over the last year:

To be clear, the company has clearly communicated that it is in the middle of a deliberate and significant reinvestment cycle, but the market is likely also watching closely to see whether or not this investment - at roughly $1.5 billion in R&D in 2025 and 4,000 engineers - actually pays off in significant future revenue growth:

In my mind, the company is at a significant transition point where AI will act as a significant revenue driver during this next phase.

This is because whether you're an existing SaaS business, or a foundation model company, all software runs on infrastructure that needs to be monitored. As the market-leading tool for accomplishing this very task, Datadog (and its new SRE agent) appears ready to capitalize as software enters a post-Claude Code world.

With the company's SRE agent growing rapidly, with over 2,000 customers now trialing or paying for the product, it shifts Datadog from purely a monitoring tool into an active operational participant, which could have higher monetization potential:

We launched Bits AI SRE Agent for general availability in December to accelerate root cause analysis and incident response. Over 2,000 trial and paying customers have run investigations in the past month, which indicates significant interest and shows great outcomes with Bits AI SRE. And we're well on our way with Bits AI Dev agent, which detects code level issues, generates fixes in production context and can even help release and monitor a fix and Bits AI Security Agent, which autonomously triages SIEM signals, conducts investigations and delivers recommendations.

When you zoom out, analysts are currently anticipating revenue growth of 20% year-over-year in 2026, 19.2% growth in 2027, and 19.6% growth in 2028:

I believe these numbers are directionally correct, and frame the broader bullish opportunity here as the company continues to expand.

Note not only that, but the company's operational margins should also improve as R&D spending slows and revenue accelerates in the coming years. With this dynamic, operating margins could grow to high single digits (or even double digits) from breakeven last year, which represents significant operational leverage, primarily powered by the competitive advantages I've discussed.

Valuation

When you add it all up, what we have here is a relatively defensible software business model, strong projected revenue growth, and significant potential margin expansion as the business unlocks operational leverage as R&D investments made in prior years mature.

Given that the business has no current profit to evaluate, I view a price-to-sales multiple as the most appropriate metric for valuation.

Over the last 12 months, Datadog trades at a price-to-sales multiple of 13.7x. Given the company's average price-to-sales ratio of 30x over the last six years, this looks like a relative discount, although I suspect that with a somewhat reduced growth profile versus years past, the multiple has shrunk to compensate.

Going forward, if the business can sustain a high-teens or low-20s revenue growth rate alongside an operating inflection, what would a fair sales multiple be?

I think a steady-state multiple of 10x sales is achievable in the years ahead. This implies a slight multiple compression versus the trailing multiple, but in a market like the one we're currently seeing, I expect that almost all software will be rated lower going forward than it currently is.

At an exit multiple of 10x sales, Datadog promises to return mid-to-high single-digit total returns over the next several years:

With a respectable, but not strong competitive moat, and an attractive but not overly exciting total returns profile, I rate shares of Datadog a 'Hold.'

Summary

While the company's sales growth appears enticing, and a strong data and workflow moat positions Datadog to survive better than some competitors in the software space, an expensive-looking sales multiple and modest total returns potential cloud the picture.

As such, I rate shares of DDOG a 'Hold.' If you have a solid cost basis in the stock, sticking around for the company's next chapter makes sense, but for those looking to allocate fresh capital, I believe there are more attractive places in the market at present.

Cheers~

data.title: China Yuchai International: Why I Don't Foresee Another Upswing In 2026
data.description:
Whenever a business receives a lot of short-term attention, that business can "spike". Sometimes the reason it receives attention is that it has "spiked". Today, I'm going to cover a relatively special type of company - China Yuchai (CYD). The reason I'm covering it is a combination of requests and my own interest. In mid-February, I got my eyes opened to the company, given some fairly significant valuation increases. Furthermore, current rating averages for Quant, for Wall Street, and for SA analysts are all positive, with either "BUY" or "STRONG BUY" being the norm here.

So what I intend to do here is to investigate whether there's actual justification for what's happening here, or if this is a "hollow" sort of upside. Furthermore, even if the upside and trend aren't hollow as such, can the company really be argued to be safe enough for investing - and if so, at what price and with what upside?

Small-cap companies like this one are interesting. I invest in a lot of them in the European markets, and cover them as well. My rule of thumb is that the smaller a company is, the more likely it seems to be overlooked by players on the market, and the deeper valuation discounts can get. The trends for CYD suggest to me that there might have been a valuation discount that has now been corrected.

So, is that the case, or is there something else going on here?

That's what we'll be looking at in this piece.

China Yuchai - Company, Structure, Appeal

I have historically been very reluctant to invest in anything Chinese. The reasons for this have to do with how these companies are listed, whether I have actual ownership in shares (as someone with a semi-legal background, the legalities of these questions are of central importance to me). And because the company's US-listed ticker is a Chinese firm via a Cayman entity, the company is a perfect example of a VIE, or variable-interest structure. While technically a holding company, I argue the risks are comparable between the two.

The issue with investing in Chinese companies is that the offshore holding company does not own the equity of the actual company doing the operations that are the focus of the company's bullish upside (Guangxi Yuchai Machinery Company Limited). It instead relies on contractual arrangements, and because Guangxi is a Chinese company, Chinese law applies for interpreting contractual agreements. Thus, the enforceability is in question, especially if the Chinese government were to restrict or ban the VIE structure currently used by most. Realize that as a VIE investor, you lack enforcement rights in Chinese courts because the offshore entity lacks ownership in the assets.

Furthermore, the Chinese entity (Guangxi) is managed by Chinese nationals. It's not outside the realm of possibility that these could be detained or face pressure from authorities, affecting operations.

The lack of control, right to profits, and the legality issues with VIE structures need to be priced for. I feel that this is something that many analysts do insufficiently, or do not do at all.

Typically, investors apply a 30-40% discount for VIE. I tend to use a similar starting point, and then look at individual risks and considerations - as I will do here.

Now, China Yuchai is not a new company. A point in its favor. What it does is manufacture diesel and natural gas engines for construction equipment, marine, buses, and trucks. It's therefore a peer to a company like Cummins (CMI), or even Volvo (VOLAF). Its main subsidiary, mentioned before as Guangxi, is the main subsidiary that does operations. It also works with new tech, like hydrogen, and works to move into clean power and power generation solutions.

Looking at the company's portfolio, we're talking about a compelling mix of hydrogen and legacy, as well as generators of essentially all sizes. The company also does things like excavators and drilling rigs.

The company is one of China's largest engine manufacturers, and it's been around for well over 70 years. It sells both to the domestic Chinese market and to the international market. It pays a dividend - $0.53 for 2025—and its key business areas as of right now are generators and engines of all sizes, alongside aftermarket support and spare parts for its main products.

The company also has a number of appealing JVs with companies in Germany and the UK, including Rolls-Royce (RYCEF). However, a quick look at the company's OEMs for its technologies tells us that the share of international business is very small, with most of its end markets being in mainland China. The same is true, aside from Truck/Bus, also for Construction machinery and Agricultural machinery.

While their company is targeting expansion into Europe, it's fair to say that they are in their infancy and only recently started targeting outside markets.

It's therefore fairer to characterize the company as a Chinese industrial/engine manufacturer that has ambitions to become international, with its Frankfurt premises being relatively new. To appeal to customers, the company is even offering "free test engines".

It should be noted that this is not the first time Yuchai has tried to appeal to the European market, but given that the last piece of news on that website is more than 5 years old. The company might have presented at Agritechnica for the first time in 2025, but has been in Europe since 2017. It's very hard to find concrete sources for sales numbers in Europe. The European managing director cites 800k units outside of China, but no clear number for Europe. When quoting specific numbers in Europe, what is cited is that "the understanding is that 5,000 tractors and other machines using Yuchai engines have been sold" in Europe, but I fail to find support for this sales number elsewhere. There are signs that Europe is expanding, with warehouse construction and R&D centers, but I'd prefer a bit more clarity on the numbers here. Since Yuchai, in the same sources, admits that the Chinese market is slowing down (something also supported by domestic data), it's likely that Yuchai's EPS growth will have to come from international markets and new segments, rather than its legacy business.

The unfortunate part of its recent performance is that the reason the company has outperformed (surge in February) is related to sales of engines to data centers. As such, we're seeing this engine manufacturer and industrial become part of the data/AI context, which, in my view, has a tendency to muddle things.

The company reported massive (and very good) 2025 results, with a 29% revenue increase and a 66%+ net income increase - better sales as well as a better product mix.

It might be true that in 2024 and parts of 2025, the company was significantly undervalued even with a VIE-discount applied. However, I don't believe this is the case any longer. I believe those who expect the company to bounce up from here to previous levels are potentially taking a very significant risk.

To say that China Yuchai doesn't always hit its targets would be an understatement.

As we can see, the 2025 fiscal year was the only time in the last 10 years that the company has offered anything except a negative earnings miss. Put another way, China Yuchai has made a trend out of negative outperformance and volatility, something echoed in earnings. While the net income has always been positive, it has been very volatile - and I would remind you that the reason for the positive results between -09 and -17 is related to significant domestic demand.

So, the company has very solid operations. I believe, after looking at its manufacturing, that the company has a very solid manufacturing base. The trick now is for the company to drive growth and make this appealing outside of China. The company has a strong distributor network worldwide to make this work.

I personally believe the company should leverage emerging markets more than it should mature markets - Africa, LATAM, and South America should be focal areas. I believe these markets have the demand for the compelling price/quality that China Yuchai seems to be able to offer.

The thesis I see for the company (and the counterpoint), is as follows.

China Yuchai seeks to leverage its existing position and production to cater to EV tech and legacy tech domestically and outside of China. However, the assumption that the company will be able to replicate its success outside of China, or inside of China with electric technologies, has multiple problems with its assumptions.

What issues/problems?

First off, there's much more pricing competition for EVs, which translates to pricing power (or lack of it). The rising adoption of EVs domestically also means reduced demand for the company's legacy products and services. When Yuchai became "larger", the company also got government subsidies (as many companies in China did - but government grants are nowhere near as plentiful these days (look at "other operating income" and the company's own filings/comments). There's also the big risk that I see - namely a slowing Chinese economy and industrial sector, which is reducing demand for the company's key products and services. Also, while the company's operating results were up, the company is spending much more on R&D these days - up over 35% in 2025 alone.

I believe this is likely to continue if the company wants to continue to be in these markets. These factors, combined with the fact that China Yuchai is a very small, domestically concentrated player compared to peers like CMI and Volvo, which already have global appeal, mean that I view the company's attractiveness as limited - and only relevant at a comparatively cheap valuation.

How I would value China Yuchai

The bullish thesis that many investors seem to have latched onto is, among other things, data center growth, arguing that the company, based on this growth potential, is still "cheap".

Let me start by addressing the price factor. On the basis of P/E, it's true that the company even today trades at a P/E of 14x P/E forward, which is lower than the sector median average for either Volvo or Cummins, close to above 30-35% higher than this. However, due to the aforementioned VIE structure, I believe the P/E perfectly represents that required discount - as such, the company is not cheap in terms of P/E even after the recent drop.

The company has very high volatility (as seen from forecast failures), and the domestic competition for even its legacy products is not small. Overall, Yuchai has not exactly been a good margin player either - its typical operating margins have been between 2% and 4%, with around 4.4% in 2025. Given that I don't see the company's 2025 numbers as long-term representative, I would give the company an average of 3%. But it really wouldn't matter, because no matter how you compare it to CMI and Volvo, their EBIT margins are better - and they're also not tied to domestic subsidies. CMI operates at least 3-4x higher, and Volvo is usually even better.

Therefore, Yuchai is neither better (margin-wise) nor likely to grow its margins near-term to somewhere where I would consider a premium warranted for its operational quality.

So, with fundamental quality impacted by scale and size, as well as VIE and domestic market and international expansions, and with operational qualities less than peers, what remains to argue for a higher valuation?

The data center argument?

I don't buy that one. There are too many indicators that the utilization rates for these facilities are well below par, with many projects built on hopeful projections rather than firm market demand. With those data centers equally demanding subsidies to be operated profitably, I view this argument as being a one-time, and hollow one at best for future results. At any rate, it's, to me, a far too uncertain argument to say that Yuchai is selling generators and assets to data centers, and this is a new major driver. In the future as well.

If you, on the other hand, as some investors are positive on data center and AI, it would of course be suitable to estimate this at a higher multiple.

As it stands, I would give the company the following calculation and target.

I won't use P/E for Yuchai - it's too cyclical even compared to our peers here outside of China, and the policy risks are not for the faint-hearted. Instead, I use a combination of PEG assumptions and EBITDA averages to give what I consider to be a fair value.

PEG assumptions are of limited value due to the volatility in growth, but I've seen citations and expectations of 20-27% per year. However, EBITDA is the premier multiple I like here. This is due to the normalization of earnings regardless of the capital structure, tax adjustment. It's a very fair, in my view, comparable metric - especially given its neutrality both to capital and to tax, while reflecting cash flow.

So, China Yuchai generates an average of $158M EBITDA per annum between 2021 and 2025, with a peak of $213.6M in 2025, and a low of $127.M in 2023. I round this off to $160M. The typical EBITDA average (multiple) for the company has been about 2.9x for 2021-2025, but has ballooned higher as of late, with 9.4x as of June 2025. I call this trend, for the time, non-recurring and not justified by history. The fact that even the spike between 5-9x EBITDA remains low compared to peers is something I don't have a problem with - we're talking about a VIE. If your argument is that the company is to trade at 9-10x EBITDA, then you'd also need to be able to answer how that can be justified with an operating margin of less than 4% (typically), not to mention cyclicality, rising R&D, regional concentration, and tech risks.

But for the sake of argument, let's go with 9-10x, doing 9.5x average. Even if I were to use this, and multiply by the above-mentioned EBITDA, that would imply a share price of $40.53/share, which is very close to the current share price. Ergo, even at the most bullish assumptions, this company is currently fully valued.

And while some analysts may argue that 'wait a minute, you forgot the company's cash position,' I have purposefully excluded any company balance sheet cash because I do not believe this represents cash available to shareholders, but cash locked away by regulatory hurdles, operational requirements (remember fixed assets and expansion), and R&D - that makes it a non-operating asset. To include the cash would imply it's readily available, which I believe is too uncertain. But by providing you with the full details, you can go ahead and make the adjustment if you so like.

However, to that EBITDA multiple and share price target, I would also apply a 35% VIE discount. That brings the highest possible fair value share price to $26.35/share for this company.

However, to be clear with you, my multiple is not 9.5x. I go much more to the historical average, and go by a 3.5x EBITDA multiple. Following the assumptions above, I therefore give the company a 35% adjusted 3.5x EBITDA average fair value of $10.5/share, with my PT for the company at around $7.5/share.

This stance and target represent a bearishness about the company's international expansion potential and a scepticism about investing in a VIE. To those that say that this is far too low - the company traded here less than 13 months back, and I view the development since then to be reflective of similar ups and downs that came in 2010, again in 2013, and once more in 2016-2017.

I therefore rate the company a "HOLD" and a rotation target.

Realize that this is very bearish, and I take a view counter to the market (and most analysts here), but I view my stance, for the scenario and potential I see, to be well-argued.

Thesis

I believe China Yuchai to be a potentially interesting, but currently overvalued stock with significant risk related to growth and forward expectations. I do not believe the "pivot" to data centers or to ESG/EV is as easy as expected, but something that will "eat away" at the company's profits and cash over time as legacy technology is slowly shifted out.

Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a fair value target of $10.5/share, which in turn comes to a $7.5/share price target. I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "HOLD" rating and wait until we see a better valuation - though this would require a substantial drop. You'd also have to accept the risks that come with VIE investments.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a price target of $7.5/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of March 2026.

data.title: Avoid S&P + Bonds, Buy Gold + Energy - George Noble
data.description:
Listen here or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Renowned investor George Noble talks investor education (0:30) Regime change; get out of S&P and bonds, get into gold and energy (4:50) Avoiding tech stocks (15:45) Blue owl in the coal mine (36:30)

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Welcome to Investing Experts, George Noble, the man, the myth, the legend. Very grateful to have you on the show. Thanks for making the time and taking the time. Thanks for joining us today.

George Noble: Thanks, Rena, long time listener, first time participant.

Rena Sherbill: First time caller. It's really great to have you. I'm gonna let you introduce yourself because I feel like you could probably do a better job than I can as opposed to listing the extraordinary things you've already accomplished.

I know you recently had a conference with one of our friends, Don Durrett, much to the moment in the zeitgeist, it was called the Best Stock Ideas Online Summit, which I think is a perfectly titled conference for these times and these days.

Maybe share with us what you, where you've been, what you're doing now, what you're focused on, why you're throwing these conferences, why you're still talking to people and sharing so much when you may not have to. I'd love a brief introduction into your journey to investing.

George Noble: Brief is difficult. So I'll try to wing it and get through this quickly. So it's not my first writer, I've got gray hair. I've been in the industry 45 years, grew up Fidelity, Pete Lynch's Assistant, ran a one-week trip around the country, had a couple of hedge funds, blah, blah, blah.

Had a spectacularly failed ETF in 2023, happy to talk about that. Lessons learned, always learn more in failure than in success. But what am I doing now and why am I here?

Now we've got a pretty good presence in social media. I've got 94,000 followers on X and I've done hundreds of podcasts and Spaces and I'm really big on investor education.

And you graciously mentioned the conference we had the other day. We had a best ideas online stock conference, 15 speakers, 15 ideas, the best of the best. This is the varsity team. You mentioned Don Durett and Tavi Costa also spoke to me about gold stocks. So we had buyside guys who were eating their own cooking, hedge fund guys pitching their best ideas.

I don't mind saying an employee of Seeking Alpha and a good mutual friend, Zachary Marx, appeared. Zach was on fire. He pitched a gold stock, as I remember. So in any event, it's the best of the best. Really high end, buyside guys, and there's some third party independent research fellows as well, many of whom actually are on Seeking Alpha.

Not this conference, but one I did last summer, J Mintzmyer showed up. J's a good friend. So the whole idea really is to democratize finance. I hate that term, but I identify with what it stands for. And this is kind of what Seeking Alpha also does. You're trying to help investors, educate investors.

And so what we did is we had online conference, 15 speakers, 15 ideas, no fireside chats, no fluff, just 20 minutes apiece, bang, bang, bang. The conference was last Wednesday. You can still get the replays. These are longer term ideas. It doesn't matter if the conference was a few days ago. $99, 15 ideas if you're not satisfied, satisfaction guaranteed. So I do these conferences. There will be another one coming up in a couple of months. I'm on X a lot with Spaces. I have a substack. Basically trying to help investors.

I was very fortunate to grow up in the 80s at Fidelity to benefit from the folks like Peter Lynch, Bruce Johnstone, George Vanderheiden, and standing on the shoulders of giants. And I'm just trying to pass on what I learned. So I think there's really a need for investor education out there. And I think the community writ large is very poorly served by a lot of the folks you see in the public square say on X, the Furus as they call them. You go and look at the mainstream financial press, CNBC, not to name names but I will, the information is compromised, there's conflicts of interest, it's infotainment as much as anything.

But people want the truth, they wanna know. They've lost trust in markets. There's a lot of bad stuff that goes on and loss of integrity, trust, in markets, governments, education, everywhere. People know in the same way that you know political parties aren't really serving them.

I don't think Wall Street's serving them very well either. And so what I'm trying to do, it's similar to what Seeking Alpha does, just doing it my own way, trying to help educate investors and I'm bringing the best of the best. I mean, these are guys I've known 20, 30, 40 years. These are guys who, it'd be common to pay 20, 30, $40,000 a pop to get their research. I'm trying to share this with the public. So that's really my mission.

Rena Sherbill: You talked about your history in the markets. And I feel like there are so many changes afoot and paradigm shifts that we've seen in the past few years, and that we have yet to see, most likely.

And yet so much has been priced into the market. What would you say about this market that you're looking at today and how are you encouraging investors to think about it?

George Noble: I think we're going through a regime change. I entered this business in 1981. It had four decades of declining interest rates, rising PE ratios. All financial assets went up in price. The bond yields were 14 % back in early 80s. So as the risk-free rate has come down, it's caused the price of everything to levitate.

And then beyond that, you had the role of central banks and governments trying to do everything they can to push up asset prices. And you go back to the peak of the madness in 2001, 2002, when we had the wake of COVID, where they threw so much money at the system. they were buying mortgage-backed securities even when 10-year was 60 basis points, it was craziness. And so we've come through a very unusual period of time.

And it's a very challenging environment, both for novices as well as veterans, especially myself. When you distort the risk-free rate, when you flood the system of liquidity, things happen. And so to put a, maybe in terms people can understand, think about real estate, think about housing. Everyone knows about houses, that's easy enough to understand. Prices kind of went to crazy levels, but there's no sellers, the price is low, mortgage rates are really low.

Well, now that's all reversing. And so I think the whole system has been turbocharged with extraordinary budget deficits and I would say reckless monetary policy.

And we're in the process of unwinding all of that. And so the only thing I can offer really is, I've seen a lot, I've got gray hair, least I got hair. We've been through unusual times and we should not extrapolate what's been happening.

History doesn't repeat itself, it rhymes. There's a temptation many people want to say, this right here, right now, how does this compare to the NASDAQ bust? Is it better, is it worse? It's different. Actually, it's much worse, but leave that aside for a second.

So I think investors really, if you only been in the market, say since the post-COVID era, the last 10 years, post-GFC era, it's been easy. Life's been good.

But this is not normal. This is not normal. And it's in process of shifting now.

I think where as previously we're in disinflationary environment and the market consistently was overestimating inflation. I think that's flipped post COVID. I think we're in an inflationary environment.

Inflation, depending on what you use to measure, it's two, three, whatever. When you have a 10 year at 4.20, can you stop and consider that the US government is running what, a $2 trillion deficit?

5 trillion in revenues, ballpark. 5 trillion revenues, 7 trillion in spending, 38 trillion in debt, 100 trillion in off balance sheet liabilities. Think about it for a second.
